risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Those who agreed to participate were randomized to either the Standard Treatment group (STD; n=49) or to the Standard Treatment + Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment group (STD+CBT; n=52) using a computerized urn randomization procedure [28]. The two conditions were balanced on gender, age, ethnic background, pain level recorded at baseline, and RDC axis I diagnoses." Comment: The paper explicitly describes a computerized urn randomization procedure, which indicates a systematic random allocation process and is sufficient to rate random sequence generation as low risk.

Quote: "Those who agreed to participate were randomized to either the Standard Treatment group (STD; n=49) or to the Standard Treatment + Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment group (STD+CBT; n=52) using a computerized urn randomization procedure [28]... The Project Coordinator entered the urn data during the intake session and informed the participants of their treatment assignments." Comment: The text explicitly states that assignment was done using a computerized urn randomization procedure, with the Project Coordinator entering data during the intake session and only then informing participants of assignments. This indicates the recruiter/assessor did not have foreknowledge of the next allocation before enrollment, so allocation concealment is judged low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The same therapists provided both of the study treatments in order to minimize therapist effects." and "Patients were told that the treatment was intended to change oral habits..." Comment: The report describes standardized, manual-driven treatments delivered by the same therapists, but it does not state that participants were blinded, nor that personnel delivering the interventions were blinded to allocation. Because the interventions (STD vs STD+CBT) were visibly different and the text does not provide explicit blinding of participants/personnel, risk from performance bias cannot be judged confidently.

Quote: "A trained M.A.-level research associate, who was not blinded to treatment condition, conducted the pretreatment and follow-up research assessments." Comment: The outcome assessor was explicitly unblinded. The main outcomes included patient-reported pain, depressive symptoms, and pain-related interference (e.g., "Ratings of pain experience... were collected using the Multidimensional Pain Inventory" and "The CES-D..."), which are subjective outcomes that could be influenced by knowledge of allocation. Because the outcomes were assessed by an unblinded research associate and include subjective measures, this domain is High risk.

Quote: "At posttreatment 88% of patients provided data, and 73% provided data at 52 weeks. Losses to follow-up were equivalent across treatment conditions." Comment: Even though the authors state losses were equivalent across groups, overall attrition by the relevant 52-week time point was 27% (73% retained), which is close to the hard 30% threshold and well above minimal loss. The paper reports ITT/mixed models, but with this degree of missing outcome data and no explicit missing-data sensitivity analysis (e.g., multiple imputation or MAR justification) provided, attrition could still bias the results.
